meta.note: Expanded AI terminology cheat sheet. Draft terms are visible for navigation and will be fully authored over time.

categories.name: AI Basics
categories.description: Start here. Understand what AI actually means.

terms.name: Artificial Intelligence (AI)
terms.tagline: The broad field of creating machines that can perform tasks that normally require human intelligence
terms.plainEnglish: Artificial Intelligence is the name for the entire field of building machines that can do things we normally think of as requiring human intelligence. This covers everything from a chess program that can beat a grandmaster to a voice assistant that understands your morning commands. The core idea is simple: instead of telling a computer exactly what to do for every possible situation, you build a system that can figure things out on its own. AI has been around as a concept since the 1950s, but it's only in the last decade that it's exploded into everyday life. That's because three things finally came together: massive amounts of data, powerful computers (especially GPUs), and smarter algorithms. Without any one of these, modern AI wouldn't work. When people talk about AI today, they're almost always talking about a specific subfield called machine learning - and more specifically, large language models (LLMs) like GPT or Claude. But AI as a field also includes things like robotics, computer vision, speech recognition, and game-playing systems. It's a big tent.
terms.analogy: Think of AI like teaching someone to cook. You could give them a recipe book with every single instruction written out (traditional programming), or you could let them watch thousands of hours of cooking videos, taste your food, get feedback, and eventually learn to cook on their own (AI). The second approach is more flexible - it can handle new ingredients or unexpected kitchen situations because it's learned the underlying principles, not just memorised recipes.
terms.howItWorks: Most modern AI systems work by finding patterns in data. A traditional computer program follows rules that a human programmer writes line by line. An AI system instead discovers its own rules by processing thousands or millions of examples. For instance, instead of writing rules like 'if the photo has fur and whiskers, classify it as a cat', you show an AI thousands of labelled cat and dog photos and let it figure out the distinguishing features on its own. This approach is powerful because it scales. A human programmer can only write so many rules, but an AI can analyse patterns across billions of data points. The trade-off is that AI systems can be opaque - even their creators often can't say exactly why the system made a particular decision. This is why the field of AI safety and explainability has become so important.
terms.inPractice:
AI is everywhere in 2026. It's in your phone (face recognition, predictive text), your email (spam filters, smart replies), your search engine (ranking results, answering questions), and increasingly your workplace (code assistants, document summarisers, customer service chatbots). Companies use AI for everything from fraud detection to drug discovery to autonomous vehicles.

Most people interact with AI indirectly  -  they don't think 'I'm using an AI', they just think 'my phone knows what I'm going to type' or 'my email sorted itself'. That invisibility is actually a sign of success: the best AI is the kind that just works without you noticing it.
keyPoints: AI learns patterns from data  -  it's not hand-coded rules for every situation
Three things enabled modern AI: massive data, powerful GPUs, and smarter algorithms
Today, 'AI' mostly means machine learning  -  specifically LLMs like GPT or Claude
AI is already everywhere: spam filters, autocomplete, face recognition, recommendations

terms.name: Machine Learning (ML)
terms.tagline: A subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed
terms.plainEnglish: Machine Learning is the subset of AI that's responsible for most of the magic you hear about. Instead of a programmer writing explicit rules for every situation, a machine learning system learns from examples. You feed it data, it finds patterns, and those patterns become its knowledge. If you've ever used a recommendation system ('customers who bought this also bought…'), that's machine learning in action. The key insight is that ML systems get better with more data. A traditional program has fixed rules - it's as good on day one as it will ever be. But an ML model can improve as you feed it more examples. This is why companies pour enormous resources into collecting data: more data usually means better models.
terms.analogy: Imagine trying to explain what a cat looks like to someone who's never seen one. You could describe it in words ('pointy ears, whiskers, furry, about the size of a loaf of bread')  -  that's traditional programming. Or you could show them a hundred photos of cats and let them figure out the pattern  -  that's machine learning. The second approach works better because cats come in all shapes, sizes, and colours, and it's nearly impossible to write a rule that covers every single one.
terms.howItWorks: Machine learning algorithms work by adjusting internal parameters to minimise errors. During training, the model makes a prediction, compares it to the correct answer, and tweaks its parameters to get closer next time. This process repeats millions of times across thousands of examples. The result is a model that has internalised the statistical patterns in the training data. There are three main flavours of machine learning. In supervised learning, you provide both inputs and correct answers (like photos labelled 'cat' or 'dog'). In unsupervised learning, you only provide inputs and let the model find structure on its own. In reinforcement learning, an agent learns by taking actions and receiving rewards or penalties, like a video game character learning to navigate a level.
terms.inPractice:
Machine Learning powers recommendation engines (Netflix, Spotify, Amazon), fraud detection (banks flagging unusual transactions), medical diagnosis (analysing X-rays and MRIs), and autonomous vehicles (identifying pedestrians, reading road signs). Most 'AI' products you use today are actually ML under the hood.

The ML workflow typically involves collecting and cleaning data, choosing an algorithm, training the model, evaluating its performance, and then deploying it for inference. Tools like scikit-learn, TensorFlow, and PyTorch have made this process accessible to millions of developers.
keyPoints: ML systems learn patterns from data, not from hand-coded rules
More data usually means better models
Three main paradigms: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning
Powers most AI products: recommendations, fraud detection, search

terms.name: Deep Learning (DL)
terms.tagline: A subset of ML using multi-layered neural networks to learn complex patterns
terms.plainEnglish: Deep Learning is a specialised branch of machine learning that uses neural networks with many layers - hence the 'deep'. While traditional machine learning often needs a human to decide which features are important (like 'the edges in this image' or 'the frequency of this word'), deep learning figures out those features on its own. You just give it raw data, and it builds up understanding layer by layer. The 'deep' in deep learning refers to the number of layers in the neural network. A shallow network might have 2-3 layers; a deep one can have hundreds. Each layer extracts increasingly abstract features. In an image recognition task, the first layer might detect edges, the next layer detects shapes, then eyes and noses, and finally full faces. The network discovers this hierarchy automatically during training.
terms.analogy: Think of deep learning like a team of art critics examining a painting. The first critic looks only at individual brushstrokes. The second looks at how those strokes form shapes. A third looks at how shapes become objects. A fourth looks at the composition as a whole. Each critic passes their analysis up the chain. No single critic sees the full picture, but together they build a complete understanding  -  and crucially, no one told them what to look for at each level; they figured it out from examples.
terms.howItWorks: Deep learning relies on neural networks - computing systems loosely inspired by the human brain. These networks consist of layers of interconnected 'neurons', each performing a simple mathematical operation. During training, the network processes millions of examples, and a technique called backpropagation adjusts the connections between neurons to reduce errors. The breakthrough that made deep learning practical was the combination of GPUs (which can perform many calculations in parallel) and large datasets. Around 2012, AlexNet showed that deep neural networks could crush traditional computer vision approaches on the ImageNet challenge. That moment kicked off the deep learning revolution. Since then, deep learning has transformed not just computer vision but also natural language processing, speech recognition, and game-playing.
terms.inPractice:
Every major AI breakthrough in the last decade  -  from GPT and DALL-E to AlphaGo and self-driving cars  -  has been powered by deep learning. It's the technology behind image recognition in Google Photos, voice assistants like Siri and Alexa, real-time translation in Google Translate, and the recommendation algorithms on YouTube and TikTok.

Training deep learning models requires significant computational resources. The largest models (like GPT-4) cost tens of millions of dollars to train and use thousands of GPUs running for weeks. However, you can use pre-trained models through APIs for pennies per query, making the power of deep learning accessible to any developer.
keyPoints: Uses multi-layered neural networks to automatically discover features from raw data
Enabled by GPUs and large datasets -- AlexNet 2012 was the breakthrough
Each layer extracts increasingly abstract patterns (edges -> shapes -> objects)
Powers GPT, DALL-E, AlphaGo, self-driving cars, and voice assistants

terms.name: Model
terms.tagline: The output of training  -  a mathematical representation that can make predictions or generate text
terms.plainEnglish: A model is the output of training - it's what you get after a machine learning algorithm has finished processing data. Think of it as a mathematical snapshot of all the patterns the AI discovered during training. Once you have a model, you can give it new inputs and it will make predictions, generate text, identify objects in images, or whatever it was trained to do. A trained model is a file - a big collection of numbers (parameters) that encode everything the AI learned. When you use ChatGPT, you're not talking to the training process; you're talking to the finished model that was produced by training and then deployed to servers. The model doesn't learn or change while you use it (unless it's designed to keep learning, which most aren't).
terms.analogy: A model is like a recipe your grandmother perfected after years of cooking. The recipe itself is the model  -  it's the distilled knowledge of 'what works.' You don't need to re-experiment every time you want to make her famous cookies; you just follow the recipe. Similarly, once a model is trained, you don't need to retrain it  -  you just use it (this is called inference).
terms.howItWorks: Technically, a model is defined by its architecture (the structure of layers and connections) and its parameters (the numerical values that determine how input data flows through the network). The architecture is designed by humans; the parameters are learned automatically during training. Models exist on a spectrum from simple to complex. A linear regression model might have just two parameters (slope and intercept) and fit on a napkin. GPT-4 is estimated to have over a trillion parameters and requires gigabytes of storage. But they're both models - they both encode learned patterns that can be applied to new data.
terms.inPractice: When you use an AI service, you're almost always using a pre-trained model. Companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Meta train enormous models and then offer access through APIs. As a developer, you can either use these existing models or train your own (or fine-tune an existing one on your specific data). The choice of model depends on your task: small models are faster and cheaper but less capable; large models are more capable but slower and more expensive. This is the model selection problem that every AI practitioner faces.
keyPoints: A trained model is a file containing all the parameters the AI learned
Processes new inputs via inference -- no further learning during use
Architecture designed by humans; parameters learned during training
Trade-off: small models are fast and cheap; large models are more capable

terms.name: Algorithm
terms.tagline: A step-by-step set of instructions that tells a computer how to solve a problem
terms.plainEnglish: An algorithm is a step-by-step recipe for solving a problem. In AI, algorithms are the procedures that govern how a model learns from data and how it makes predictions. If the model is the finished product, the algorithm is the manufacturing process and the instruction manual combined. You encounter algorithms everywhere in daily life: a recipe for baking a cake is an algorithm; your GPS's route-finding logic is an algorithm; the process your email provider uses to detect spam is an algorithm. In AI, the term usually refers to the mathematical procedure that the training process follows - things like gradient descent, backpropagation, or the transformer architecture.
terms.analogy: An algorithm is like a cooking recipe. It tells you: (1) preheat the oven to 350°F, (2) mix flour, sugar, and eggs, (3) bake for 30 minutes. If you follow the steps correctly, you get a cake. In AI, the algorithm tells the computer: (1) initialise the parameters randomly, (2) feed it a batch of training data, (3) calculate the error, (4) adjust parameters to reduce error, (5) repeat. Follow the steps, you get a trained model.
terms.howItWorks: Different algorithms are suited for different types of problems. Linear regression finds a straight-line relationship between variables. Decision trees create if-then rules. Neural networks use layers of connected nodes. Gradient descent is the workhorse algorithm that most neural networks use to learn - it calculates which direction to adjust parameters to reduce errors and takes a step in that direction. The choice of algorithm dramatically affects how well a model performs and how long training takes. There's no single 'best' algorithm - it depends on your data, your problem, and your constraints. This is why the field of hyperparameter tuning exists: finding the right settings for your chosen algorithm.
terms.inPractice:
When you train a model using a framework like PyTorch or TensorFlow, you're selecting and configuring algorithms. The framework handles the low-level implementation; you choose things like the optimiser (Adam, SGD), the loss function, and the learning rate. Understanding what different algorithms do helps you make better choices and debug when things go wrong.

Many modern AI systems are 'algorithm-agnostic' from a user's perspective  -  you don't need to know which algorithm powers ChatGPT to use it effectively. But understanding algorithms helps you reason about why a model behaves the way it does.
keyPoints: An algorithm is the step-by-step procedure the model follows to learn
Examples: gradient descent, backpropagation, decision trees, linear regression
No single best algorithm -- choice depends on data, problem, and constraints
Frameworks like PyTorch handle implementation; you choose and configure the algorithm

facts.label: Algorithm ≠ Model
facts.value: The algorithm is the procedure; the model is the result

terms.name: Inference
terms.tagline: When a trained model runs on new input to produce an output (prediction, text, image)
terms.plainEnglish: Inference is what happens when you use a trained model. You give it new input, and it produces output - a prediction, a generated sentence, a classification. If training is the learning phase, inference is the using phase. Every time you ask ChatGPT a question and get an answer, you're running inference on GPT's model. Inference is dramatically cheaper and faster than training. Training GPT-4 cost tens of millions of dollars and took months. Running a single inference - like asking it a question - costs fractions of a penny and takes seconds. This is the whole business model of AI companies: they invest heavily in training once, then charge small amounts per inference.
terms.analogy: Think of inference like a doctor diagnosing a patient. The doctor's training (medical school, residency, years of experience) is the training phase. When a patient walks in with symptoms and the doctor makes a diagnosis, that's inference. The training took years, but each diagnosis takes minutes. The doctor doesn't need to redo medical school for each patient  -  they apply what they already learned.
terms.howItWorks: During inference, input data flows forward through the model's network (this is called the forward pass). Each layer transforms the data slightly, applying the mathematical operations encoded in its parameters. By the time the data reaches the final layer, it has been transformed into the desired output format - whether that's a probability ('87% chance this is a cat'), a token in a sentence, or a classification label. Inference can run on different hardware depending on the use case. For real-time applications like chatbots, inference happens on powerful cloud servers (GPUs or custom AI chips). For on-device applications (like your phone's face unlock or keyboard predictions), inference runs locally on a specialised chip in your device. There's a growing push toward on-device inference for privacy and latency benefits.
terms.inPractice: Inference is what you're paying for when you use an AI API. Services charge per token processed - both input and output tokens count. This is why inference cost is a major consideration when building AI applications. Techniques like caching, batching, and model distillation exist to make inference cheaper and faster. The speed of inference is measured in latency (time per request) and throughput (requests per second). Different use cases need different trade-offs: a self-driving car needs extremely low latency (milliseconds), while a batch data-processing job can tolerate longer inference times.
keyPoints: Inference is using a trained model on new input to get an output
Dramatically cheaper than training -- fractions of a penny vs millions of dollars
Measured by latency (time per request) and throughput (requests per second)
Can run on cloud GPUs or on-device chips for privacy and speed

facts.label: Inference hardware
facts.value: GPUs, TPUs, or specialised AI chips

facts.label: On-device inference
facts.value: Runs locally for privacy and speed

terms.name: Parameter
terms.tagline: A value inside a model that is learned during training and determines how the model behaves
terms.plainEnglish: Parameters are the internal numbers inside a model that determine how it behaves. During training, the model adjusts these numbers to better match the patterns in its training data. Think of parameters as the model's 'knowledge' - they're the learned values that encode what the model knows. The size of a model is often described by its number of parameters. '70B model' means 70 billion parameters. More parameters generally means more capacity to learn complex patterns, but it also means more computing power needed for both training and inference. The relationship isn't perfectly linear - a well-designed smaller model can sometimes outperform a sloppy larger one.
terms.analogy: Parameters are like the knobs and dials on a massive sound mixing board. Each knob controls something tiny  -  the volume of one specific frequency in one specific channel. Individually, no single knob matters much. But when all 70 billion knobs are set just right, the result is beautiful music. Training is the process of tweaking every knob until the output sounds right. The final settings are the trained parameters.
terms.howItWorks: In a neural network, parameters come in two varieties: weights and biases. Weights determine the strength of connections between neurons - how much one neuron's output influences another's. Biases are offset values that give each neuron a threshold to activate. During training, both weights and biases are adjusted through backpropagation and gradient descent. Parameters start as random values and gradually converge to meaningful ones. A single parameter on its own encodes nothing meaningful - it's the collective configuration of all parameters working together that creates the model's capabilities. This is why interpreting what a model 'knows' by looking at individual parameters is impossible.
terms.inPractice:
When choosing a model, parameter count is a rough proxy for capability. Small models (1-7B parameters) run on consumer hardware and work well for simple tasks. Medium models (8-70B) are the workhorses of the industry. Large models (100B+) offer the best performance but require significant infrastructure.

However, parameter count isn't everything. A model's architecture, training data quality, and training method all matter enormously. A well-trained 7B model can outperform a poorly-trained 70B model on specific tasks. This is why benchmarks are more useful than parameter counts for comparing models.
keyPoints: Parameters are the internal numbers that encode what a model knows
Two types in neural networks: weights (connection strength) and biases (thresholds)
More parameters = more capacity, but also more compute needed
Parameter count is a rough proxy for capability, not the whole story

terms.name: Training
terms.tagline: The process of teaching a model by showing it examples and adjusting its parameters
terms.plainEnglish: Training is the process where you teach a model by showing it examples and letting it adjust its parameters. It's the most computationally expensive and time-consuming part of building an AI. During training, the model starts knowing nothing - its parameters are random - and gradually learns to produce useful outputs. The goal of training is to find the set of parameters that minimises error on the training data while also generalising well to new data. This is a balancing act: train too little and the model doesn't learn enough; train too much and it might just memorise the training examples (overfitting). Knowing when to stop is a key skill.
terms.analogy: Training is like learning a new language through immersion. At first, you understand nothing. You hear sentences (input), guess what they mean (prediction), get corrected (error calculation), and adjust your understanding (parameter update). After thousands of interactions, you start to get it right. The 'training' never shows you every possible sentence  -  you learn enough general rules to handle new sentences you've never heard before.
terms.howItWorks: Training follows a loop: (1) feed the model a batch of examples, (2) let it make predictions, (3) compare predictions to the correct answers using a loss function, (4) calculate how much each parameter contributed to the error (backpropagation), (5) adjust all parameters slightly to reduce future errors (gradient descent). Repeat millions of times across thousands of batches. One complete pass through the entire training dataset is called an epoch. Models typically train for multiple epochs. The learning rate determines how big each adjustment is - too big and the model never settles on good values; too small and training takes forever. Modern training runs use sophisticated optimisers like Adam that automatically adjust learning rates during training.
terms.inPractice: Training a large model is a major engineering undertaking. GPT-4's training reportedly cost over $100 million in compute time. But you don't need to train from scratch - most practitioners use pre-trained models and optionally fine-tune them on their own data, which is much cheaper. Training requires three datasets: a training set (for learning), a validation set (for tuning hyperparameters), and a test set (for final evaluation). This split prevents you from accidentally cheating by letting the model see test data during training. The training process produces checkpoints - snapshots of the model at various points - so you can resume if something crashes.
keyPoints: Training adjusts parameters to minimise error on training examples
Follows a loop: predict -> compare to answer -> backpropagate -> adjust
Requires three datasets: training, validation, and test sets
Most practitioners fine-tune pre-trained models rather than training from scratch

terms.name: Dataset
terms.tagline: A collection of examples used to train, validate, or test a model
terms.plainEnglish: A dataset is a collection of examples used to teach or evaluate an AI model. If the model is the student, the dataset is the textbook. The quality, size, and diversity of your dataset largely determine how well your model will perform. As the saying goes in AI: garbage in, garbage out. Datasets can be almost anything: millions of photos labelled 'cat' or 'dog', billions of text documents from the internet, thousands of hours of recorded speech, or medical records with diagnoses. For supervised learning, each example in the dataset needs a label - the correct answer. For unsupervised learning, the raw data alone is enough.
terms.analogy: Think of a dataset like a stack of flashcards for a student. Each flashcard has a question (input) and an answer (label). The student studies thousands of flashcards until they can answer any question correctly. If the flashcards only cover addition, the student won't learn subtraction  -  your dataset determines what your model can learn. And if some flashcards have wrong answers, the student learns the wrong thing.
terms.howItWorks: Building a good dataset is often the hardest part of AI development. The dataset needs to be representative of the real world, large enough to cover edge cases, and clean (free of errors and contradictions). A biased dataset produces a biased model - if you train a hiring AI mostly on male candidates' resumes, it will be worse at evaluating female candidates. Datasets are typically split into three subsets: training set (for learning), validation set (for tuning), and test set (for final evaluation). A common split is 80/10/10 or 70/15/15. The test set must never be used during training - it's held back to give an honest measure of how well the model generalises.
terms.inPractice:
Public datasets power much of AI research: ImageNet (14 million labelled images), Common Crawl (billions of web pages), Wikipedia, and specialised datasets for everything from medical imaging to chess games. Many companies build proprietary datasets from their user data  -  this is often their competitive advantage.

Data quality matters more than quantity. A carefully curated dataset of 10,000 high-quality examples can outperform a noisy dataset of 1 million examples. This is why data cleaning, deduplication, and annotation are such important parts of the AI pipeline.
keyPoints: A dataset is the collection of examples used to teach or evaluate a model
Quality and diversity matter more than sheer quantity
Biased datasets produce biased models -- representative data is critical
Common splits: training (80%), validation (10%), test (10%)

terms.name: Feature
terms.tagline: An individual measurable input variable used by a model to make predictions
terms.plainEnglish: A feature is an individual measurable input that a model uses to make a prediction. In traditional machine learning, humans hand-pick the features - things like 'number of bedrooms' for a house price predictor or 'word frequency' for a spam detector. In deep learning, the model discovers its own features from raw data, which is both the power and the mystery of modern AI. Think of features as the clues a detective uses to solve a case. The 'short, bearded man in a red hat' - each attribute is a feature. More useful features mean a better prediction. The art of traditional ML was feature engineering: figuring out which measurements would be most predictive for your problem.
terms.analogy: Imagine you're trying to predict whether a fruit is an apple or an orange. You might measure features like colour, weight, firmness, and diameter. Colour alone isn't enough (some apples are green, some are red), but combined with the other features, you can make a good prediction. Features are those individual measurements  -  each one provides a small piece of evidence, and together they tell the full story.
terms.howItWorks:
In traditional machine learning, features are often represented as a vector of numbers  -  one number per feature. A house prediction model might have features like [square_feet, bedrooms, bathrooms, age, location_score]. Each house becomes a point in this 'feature space'. The model learns how each feature correlates with the target value.

In deep learning, the model automatically extracts hierarchical features. The first layers detect simple patterns (edges in images, letter combinations in text). Deeper layers combine these into complex features (faces, concepts). This automatic feature discovery is why deep learning works so well on raw data like pixels or text characters  -  it doesn't need a human to pre-digest the information.
terms.inPractice:
Feature engineering was the dominant paradigm in ML before deep learning took over. Data scientists would spend weeks creating and testing features. While deep learning has reduced the need for manual feature engineering, features remain crucial in many contexts: structured data (spreadsheets, databases), time-series forecasting, and any situation where you have expert domain knowledge.

In modern LLMs, 'features' aren't hand-crafted  -  the model learns representations internally. But understanding the concept of features helps you reason about what your model is doing and debug poor performance. If your house-price model only uses square footage and ignores location, it will make bad predictions  -  it has the wrong features.
keyPoints: A feature is a measurable input variable a model uses to make predictions
Traditional ML requires manual feature engineering; deep learning discovers features automatically
Features are represented as numerical vectors in model input
Understanding features helps debug poor model performance

terms.name: Label
terms.tagline: The correct answer or target value that a supervised model learns to predict
terms.plainEnglish: A label is the correct answer for a given input in supervised learning. When you train a model to recognise cats, every photo needs a label - 'cat' or 'not cat'. The model learns by comparing its predictions to these labels and adjusting to get closer. Labels are what turn raw data into a teaching tool. Labels are usually created by humans - a process called annotation or labelling. This is how companies like Scale AI make money: they employ thousands of people worldwide to label data. Without labelled data, supervised learning wouldn't work. It's the fuel that powers most practical AI.
terms.analogy: Labels are like the answer key at the back of a textbook. Without the answer key, you can do all the practice problems but never know if you're right or wrong. The answer key lets you check your work, see where you went wrong, and improve. The model does the same thing  -  it makes a prediction, checks the label, and adjusts.
terms.howItWorks:
Labels can be categorical (cat/dog, spam/not-spam), numerical (price of a house at $450,000), or complex (segmentation masks that outline every object in an image). The format depends on the task. For classification, labels are usually one-hot encoded vectors. For text generation, labels are the next word or token in a sequence.

Creating high-quality labels is expensive and difficult. Different human annotators might disagree on the same data (is this email spam or not?), leading to 'noisy labels'. Training on noisy labels degrades model performance. This is why annotation guidelines are meticulously detailed, and why some organisations use multiple annotators per datapoint to measure agreement.
terms.inPractice: The cost and difficulty of labelling is a major bottleneck in AI. Labelling a single medical image might require a specialist doctor. Labelling a 3D point cloud for a self-driving car can take hours. This has driven interest in techniques like semi-supervised learning (using a few labels plus lots of unlabelled data), weak supervision (using heuristics to generate noisy labels automatically), and synthetic data.
keyPoints: A label is the correct answer for a given input in supervised learning
Labels are created by human annotators -- the major bottleneck in AI
Labels can be categorical, numerical, or complex (like image segmentation)
Noisy labels (human disagreement) reduce model performance

terms.name: Supervised Learning
terms.tagline: Training where the model learns from input-output pairs (labeled data)
terms.plainEnglish: Supervised learning is the most common type of machine learning. You provide the model with input-output pairs - examples where the correct answer is already known - and the model learns to map inputs to outputs. It's called 'supervised' because the training data provides the answers, like a teacher supervising a student. Virtually every AI application you've used relies heavily on supervised learning. Spam filters were trained on emails labelled 'spam' or 'not spam'. Facial recognition was trained on photos labelled with people's names. Voice assistants were trained on recordings paired with their text transcripts. If there's labelled data involved, it's probably supervised learning.
terms.analogy: Supervised learning is like a student learning math from a textbook with an answer key. The student tries a problem (input), checks the answer key (label), and figures out where they went wrong. After enough practice problems, they can solve new problems they've never seen before. The teacher (supervisor) provides the answers, but the student does the learning.
terms.howItWorks: The supervised learning process is straightforward: (1) collect a dataset of input-output pairs, (2) split it into training, validation, and test sets, (3) choose a model architecture, (4) train the model minimising error on the training set, (5) evaluate on the test set to measure true performance. Two major categories of supervised learning are classification (predicting a category - 'cat' or 'dog') and regression (predicting a number - 'house price: $450,000'). The loss function differs: cross-entropy loss for classification, mean squared error for regression. But the core principle - learning from labelled examples - is the same.
terms.inPractice: Supervised learning requires labelled data, which is expensive to produce. Modern approaches often combine supervised learning with other techniques: pre-training on unlabelled data (unsupervised) followed by fine-tuning on labelled data (supervised). This hybrid approach, used by GPT and other LLMs, gets the best of both worlds: massive general knowledge from unlabelled data plus task-specific accuracy from labels.
keyPoints: The most common ML paradigm: learns from input-output (labelled) pairs
Two main task types: classification (categories) and regression (numbers)
Requires expensive labelled data -- the major bottleneck
Modern approach: unsupervised pre-training + supervised fine-tuning

terms.name: Unsupervised Learning
terms.tagline: Training where the model finds patterns in data without any labels
terms.plainEnglish: Unsupervised learning is training where the model receives no labels at all. It's given raw data and told to find structure on its own. This sounds harder (and in some ways it is), but it's also more scalable because unlabelled data is everywhere - every webpage, every photo, every recording is unlabelled data waiting to be used. Without labels to guide it, the model looks for patterns: which items cluster together? Which features co-occur? What's the underlying structure of the data? This makes unsupervised learning ideal for exploration, discovery, and situations where labelling is impractical. The dominant paradigm in modern AI - self-supervised learning - is a specialised form of unsupervised learning that creates its own labels from the data.
terms.analogy: Unsupervised learning is like giving someone a massive pile of Lego bricks and asking them to organise it. They don't have instructions (no labels). But they notice that red 2x4 bricks cluster together, wheel pieces are similar, and window frames form a group. They discover structure without anyone telling them what the categories are. The organisation they come up with might surprise you  -  but it reveals real patterns in the data.
terms.howItWorks: Common unsupervised learning tasks include clustering (grouping similar items together), dimensionality reduction (compressing data while preserving important structure), and density estimation (modelling the probability distribution of the data). Algorithms like k-means, DBSCAN, and PCA are classic unsupervised tools. The biggest breakthrough in unsupervised learning is self-supervised learning, where the model creates its own supervision signal from the data. For example, you can mask out words in a sentence and train the model to predict them - no human labels needed. This is how BERT and GPT learned language understanding from massive unlabelled text corpora. The 'unsupervised' pre-training phase is what gives modern LLMs their broad knowledge.
terms.inPractice:
Unsupervised learning is used for customer segmentation (grouping users by behaviour), anomaly detection (finding unusual transactions), recommendation systems (finding similar products), and as the pre-training phase for virtually every modern LLM. The 'pre-training' in 'pre-trained model' is almost always unsupervised or self-supervised.

The trade-off is that unsupervised learning produces less precise results than supervised learning for specific tasks. It's great for exploration and general understanding, but if you need a model that can reliably distinguish cats from dogs, you'll want to fine-tune with labelled data.
keyPoints: Finds patterns in unlabelled data -- no human labels needed
Self-supervised learning creates its own labels from the data itself
Used for clustering, anomaly detection, recommendations, and LLM pre-training
Less precise than supervised for specific tasks but far more scalable

facts.label: Self-supervised learning
facts.value: Creates its own labels from data

terms.name: Reinforcement Learning
terms.tagline: Training where an agent learns by taking actions and receiving rewards or penalties
terms.plainEnglish: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a training paradigm where an agent learns by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards or penalties. It's trial-and-error learning with a scoreboard. The agent doesn't know what to do at first - it tries random actions, sees what happens, and gradually figures out which actions lead to good outcomes. RL is responsible for some of AI's most spectacular achievements. DeepMind's AlphaGo beat the world champion at Go - a game so complex that traditional AI approaches couldn't touch it. RL-powered robots learn to walk, RL systems manage traffic lights, and RL even helped discover more efficient chip layouts. It excels in situations where you can define what 'good' looks like but can't easily specify how to achieve it.
terms.analogy: Think of RL like training a dog with treats. When the dog sits on command, it gets a treat (reward). When it jumps on the couch, it gets a scolding (penalty). Over time, the dog figures out which behaviours earn treats. The dog wasn't programmed to sit  -  it discovered that sitting leads to good things. RL is the same idea: the algorithm discovers strategies through trial and error, guided by rewards.
terms.howItWorks: RL has four key components: the agent (what's learning), the environment (the world it interacts with), actions (what it can do), and rewards (feedback signals). The agent's goal is to maximise cumulative reward over time. This creates interesting dynamics: sometimes the agent must sacrifice short-term reward for long-term gain (exploration vs. exploitation). A key concept is the value function - the agent's estimate of how good a particular state or action is. Deep RL combines RL with deep neural networks, allowing the agent to handle complex, high-dimensional environments (like video game screens). The agent learns not just which actions work, but why they work, enabling it to generalise to new situations.
terms.inPractice: RL requires enormous amounts of experience. AlphaGo played millions of games against itself to master Go. A robot learning to grasp objects might need thousands of real-world attempts. This makes RL expensive and slow compared to supervised learning. However, advances in simulation (training in virtual environments) and transfer learning (applying simulated skills to the real world) are making RL more practical. RL is also used in RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback), which fine-tunes language models to produce outputs humans prefer. This is a key step in making LLMs helpful, harmless, and honest.
keyPoints: An agent learns by taking actions and receiving rewards or penalties
Key components: agent, environment, actions, and rewards
AlphaGo and RLHF are famous RL applications
Requires enormous amounts of experience; often trained in simulated environments

facts.label: Key differentiator
facts.value: Learns from rewards, not labelled data

terms.name: Classification
terms.tagline: A task where the model predicts which category something belongs to
terms.plainEnglish: Classification is a supervised learning task where the model predicts which category something belongs to. Is this email spam or not? Is this photo a cat, dog, or rabbit? Is this transaction fraudulent or legitimate? These are all classification problems. The model outputs a probability for each possible category and picks the most likely one. Classification is one of the most common AI tasks. It powers email spam filters, medical diagnosis from X-rays, facial recognition, sentiment analysis (positive/negative/neutral), and content moderation. Any time you need to sort things into buckets, classification is probably the right approach.
terms.analogy: Classification is like a sorting machine at a recycling plant. Items come down a conveyor belt, and the machine sorts them into bins: plastic, glass, paper, metal. The machine looks at features (colour, shape, weight) to decide which bin each item goes in. It's rarely 100% sure  -  some items are tricky  -  but it assigns a confidence score and makes its best guess.
terms.howItWorks: Classification models output probabilities for each class, typically using a softmax activation function in the final layer. Softmax converts raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1. If a model outputs [cat: 0.92, dog: 0.07, rabbit: 0.01], it's 92% confident it's a cat. Binary classification has two categories (spam/not-spam). Multi-class classification has three or more mutually exclusive categories (cat, dog, rabbit - it can only be one). Multi-label classification allows an item to belong to multiple categories (a photo might contain both a cat AND a dog). The loss function for classification is usually cross-entropy loss, which penalises the model more when it's confidently wrong.
terms.inPractice: Classification models are evaluated using metrics like accuracy (what fraction were correct?), precision (when the model says 'spam', how often is it right?), and recall (what fraction of actual spam did the model catch?). Different applications prioritise different metrics - a cancer detection system might favour recall (don't miss any cancers) even at the cost of false positives. Most classification systems don't just return a single answer - they return confidence scores that let you decide how to act. For low-confidence predictions, you might route them to a human reviewer instead of trusting the model.
keyPoints: Predicts which category something belongs to (spam/not-spam, cat/dog)
Outputs probabilities via softmax -- the highest is the prediction
Types: binary (2 categories), multi-class (3+), multi-label (multiple allowed)
Evaluated on accuracy, precision, and recall -- not just accuracy alone

terms.name: Regression
terms.tagline: A task where the model predicts a numerical value
terms.plainEnglish: Regression is a supervised learning task where the model predicts a numerical value instead of a category. How much will this house sell for? What will the temperature be tomorrow? How many customers will visit this store? While classification answers 'which one?', regression answers 'how much?' or 'how many?'. Regression is everywhere in business and science. It's used for forecasting sales, predicting stock prices (with limited success!), estimating insurance risk, calculating the optimal price for a product, and modelling how variables relate to each other. Linear regression - finding a straight-line relationship - is often the first algorithm students learn.
terms.analogy: Think of regression like a chef estimating cooking time based on the weight of a turkey. The chef knows that a 10-pound turkey takes about 3 hours, and a 20-pound turkey takes about 5 hours. Based on these data points, they can predict that a 15-pound turkey will take about 4 hours. They're not putting the turkey in a category ('big' or 'small')  -  they're predicting a specific number.
terms.howItWorks: The simplest form of regression is linear regression, which finds the line of best fit through data points. The line is defined by an equation: y = mx + b, where 'm' is the slope and 'b' is the intercept. The model learns 'm' and 'b' to minimise the distance between predicted and actual values. Real-world regression is usually more complex. Multiple regression uses many input features. Polynomial regression fits curves instead of straight lines. Neural networks can perform highly non-linear regression, capturing complex relationships. The loss function is typically mean squared error (MSE) or mean absolute error (MAE), which measure how far predictions are from true values.
terms.inPractice: Regression models are evaluated using metrics like R-squared (what fraction of variance is explained?), RMSE (root mean squared error), and MAE (mean absolute error). These help you understand not just whether the model is 'right', but how far off it typically is. A critical warning: regression finds correlations, not causation. Ice cream sales and drowning incidents both increase in summer - a regression model would find a relationship, but buying ice cream doesn't cause drowning. Understanding this distinction is crucial for using regression responsibly.
keyPoints: Predicts a numerical value (price, temperature, count)
Simplest form: linear regression (y = mx + b)
Evaluated on R-squared, RMSE, and MAE
Finds correlations, not causation -- a critical distinction

terms.name: Overfitting
terms.tagline: When a model memorises training data instead of learning general patterns, performing poorly on new data
terms.plainEnglish: Overfitting is when a model memorises the training data instead of learning the underlying patterns. It's like a student who memorises the answers to specific practice problems but can't solve a new problem that tests the same concept. The model performs brilliantly on its training data but fails on new, unseen data. Overfitting is one of the biggest challenges in machine learning. It's easy to spot: your model gets 99% accuracy on the training set but only 60% on the test set. The gap between training performance and test performance is the telltale sign. A model that's overfitted hasn't truly learned - it's just become very good at recognising specific examples it's already seen.
terms.analogy: Imagine a student preparing for a maths exam by memorising the answer key to every problem in the textbook. On the exam, the teacher uses slightly different numbers. The student who memorised answers fails, while the student who understood the underlying concepts passes. The memoriser 'overfitted' to the textbook. The model that understands the concept 'generalises'.
terms.howItWorks: Overfitting happens when a model is too complex relative to the amount of training data. A model with millions of parameters trained on only a thousand examples has enough capacity to memorise every single example, noise and all. The model learns not just the true signal but also the random quirks and errors in the training data. Several techniques combat overfitting. Regularisation adds a penalty for complex models. Dropout randomly disables neurons during training, forcing the network to be robust. Early stopping halts training before the model starts memorising. Cross-validation helps detect overfitting by testing on multiple data splits. Most importantly, more data is the best cure for overfitting - with enough examples, memorisation becomes impossible.
terms.inPractice: When you're training a model, watching the training and validation loss curves is essential. If training loss keeps decreasing but validation loss starts increasing, you're overfitting - stop training. This is why early stopping is one of the most widely used techniques. Overfitting isn't always bad. Sometimes an 'overfitted' model on a narrow, stable domain works perfectly fine in practice. The key is knowing when it matters. If your model needs to generalise to new situations (most applications), overfitting is your enemy. If it only ever sees data identical to its training distribution, it might not matter.
keyPoints: Model memorises training data instead of learning general patterns
Telltale sign: high training accuracy, low test accuracy
Caused by a model too complex for the amount of training data
Prevented by regularisation, dropout, early stopping, and more data

facts.label: The telltale sign
facts.value: High training accuracy, low test accuracy

facts.label: Primary cause
facts.value: Model too complex for the data

terms.name: Generalisation
terms.tagline: A model's ability to perform well on data it has never seen before
terms.plainEnglish: Generalisation is a model's ability to perform well on data it has never seen before. It's the whole point of training - you don't care how well the model does on the training data; you care how well it does in the real world on new examples. A model that generalises well has learned the true underlying patterns, not just memorised specific examples. Generalisation is what separates genuine intelligence from rote memorisation. A model that's seen every possible scenario doesn't need to generalise - but in the real world, you'll always encounter new situations. The ability to handle novelty is what makes AI useful. Every time ChatGPT answers a question it wasn't specifically trained on, that's generalisation in action.
terms.analogy: Generalisation is like learning to ride a bicycle. You learn on a specific bike, in a specific park, on a specific day. But once you've learned, you can ride any bike, in any park, in any weather. You've extracted the general skill of 'bicycle riding' rather than memorising the specific experience of that one ride. AI generalisation is the same principle: extracting general rules from specific examples.
terms.howItWorks: Generalisation depends on the model finding the underlying signal in the data while ignoring the noise. The signal is the true relationship between inputs and outputs (the pattern that holds across all examples). Noise is random variation specific to individual examples (measurement error, outliers, random chance). The bias-variance trade-off captures the tension in generalisation. A model with high bias is too simple and misses the signal entirely (underfitting). A model with high variance is too complex and chases the noise (overfitting). The best generalisation comes from finding the sweet spot: complex enough to capture the signal, simple enough to ignore the noise.
terms.inPractice: You measure generalisation by evaluating the model on a held-out test set - data that was never used during training. This gives an honest estimate of real-world performance. Techniques like k-fold cross-validation provide more robust generalisation estimates by testing on multiple different splits. In modern LLMs, generalisation is both remarkable and mysterious. These models can answer questions, write code, and translate languages they were never explicitly trained on. But they also fail in surprising ways, revealing gaps in their generalisation. Understanding when and why models generalise (or fail to) is one of the most active research areas in AI.
keyPoints: A model's ability to perform well on never-before-seen data
The whole point of training -- measured on a held-out test set
Bias-variance trade-off: too simple (underfit) vs too complex (overfit)
LLMs show remarkable but mysterious generalisation abilities

terms.name: Narrow AI
terms.tagline: AI that is designed to perform one specific task (most AI today is narrow)
terms.plainEnglish: Narrow AI - also called weak AI - refers to AI systems that are designed and trained for one specific task. Your email spam filter is narrow AI: it's brilliant at identifying spam and completely useless at everything else. Google Maps is narrow AI: it's great at route planning and can't write a poem. Every AI system that exists today is narrow AI. This might seem disappointing - aren't we supposed to be building truly intelligent machines? But narrow AI is incredibly powerful within its domain. A chess AI can beat any human but can't hold a conversation. A medical diagnosis AI can spot cancer in X-rays but can't book your appointment. These systems are narrow but world-class at what they do.
terms.analogy: Narrow AI is like a world-class chef who is incredible at making pasta but has never made a single other dish in their life. Order carbonara? Perfect. Order a burger? The chef wouldn't know where to start. The chef is a 'narrow' expert  -  unbeatable in their specialty, helpless outside it. Every AI today is this chef.
terms.howItWorks:
Narrow AI succeeds by focusing on a constrained problem. The fewer variables and edge cases, the better the AI can perform. A chess AI only needs to understand chess  -  it doesn't need common sense, language understanding, or social skills. This focus allows narrow systems to exceed human performance in their domain while being completely incapable outside it.

The term 'narrow' isn't pejorative  -  it's descriptive. Most valuable AI applications are narrow by design. You don't want your self-driving car getting distracted trying to write poetry while navigating traffic. Narrow AI is reliable, predictable, and practical.
terms.inPractice:
Every AI product you've ever used is narrow AI. ChatGPT is narrow (it does language  -  and only one type of language task at a time). DALL-E is narrow (it generates images from text). Siri is narrow (a collection of narrow AIs working together). Tesla's Autopilot is narrow. DeepMind's AlphaFold (which predicts protein structures) is narrow.

The goal of most AI research is to push the boundaries of narrow systems  -  making them broader, more flexible, and more capable. But as of 2026, there is no known system that comes close to general intelligence. All AI is narrow AI. This is why the next term  -  AGI  -  is such a big deal.
keyPoints: AI designed for one specific task -- every AI system that exists today
Also called 'weak AI', though it can exceed human performance in its domain
Examples: spam filters, chess AI, ChatGPT, self-driving car subsystems
Narrow focus is a strength: these systems are reliable and predictable

terms.name: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)
terms.tagline: Hypothetical AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can
terms.plainEnglish: Artificial General Intelligence - AGI - is the hypothetical AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can. Unlike narrow AI (which can only do one thing), an AGI would be flexible, adaptive, and capable of learning new skills the way humans do. It's the 'AI' from science fiction - the kind that can hold a conversation, write a novel, solve a scientific problem, and then cook you dinner. AGI does not exist. No one has built it, and there's significant disagreement about when (or if) it will be achieved. Estimates range from 'within 5 years' to 'never' depending on who you ask. What makes AGI different from today's AI is generality - an AGI could transfer knowledge across domains, reason about novel situations, and learn new tasks without additional training.
terms.analogy: Think of the difference between a calculator and a mathematician. A calculator can do arithmetic faster and more accurately than any human  -  but it can't do anything else. That's narrow AI. A mathematician can solve equations, but they can also write papers, teach students, collaborate with colleagues, and come up with new theorems. That's general intelligence. We've built incredible calculators; we haven't built a mathematician yet.
terms.howItWorks: Nobody knows exactly how to build AGI. The leading approaches include: (1) scaling up current LLM architectures (more data, more parameters, more compute), (2) architectural innovations (new designs that overcome current limitations), (3) neuro-symbolic AI (combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning), and (4) embodied AI (AI that learns by interacting with the physical world). A major debate in the field is whether today's LLMs are showing 'glimmers' of general intelligence or just very sophisticated pattern matching. Researchers point to emergent abilities - unexpected capabilities that appear when models reach certain scales - as evidence that generalisation might be an inevitable consequence of scale. Sceptics argue that these systems lack true understanding, reasoning, and common sense.
terms.inPractice: While AGI doesn't exist yet, its pursuit shapes the entire AI industry. Companies race toward it, researchers debate it, and policymakers worry about regulating it. The potential impact of AGI - both positive and negative - is so enormous that it's spawned an entire field of AI safety focused on ensuring that if AGI is built, it's aligned with human values. Most AI practitioners focus on narrow AI, but the long-term vision of AGI drives fundamental research. Whether AGI arrives in 5 years or 50 years, the work being done today - on scaling, alignment, reasoning, and robustness - is building toward it. The question of AGI isn't just technical; it's philosophical, ethical, and perhaps the most important question of our time.
keyPoints: Hypothetical AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can
Does not exist yet -- median expert estimate for arrival is ~2045
Key challenge: generality (transferring knowledge across domains)
Its pursuit drives AI safety research and long-term AI strategy

categories.name: Neural Networks
categories.description: The brain-inspired architecture behind modern AI.

terms.name: Neural Network
terms.tagline: Layers of tiny math units wired together, trained by adjusting how much each one matters
terms.plainEnglish:
OK, so a neural network. The name makes it sound like something biological, but really it's just a bunch of tiny math operations wired together. The basic building block is called a "neuron," which is a misleading name  -  it's a few lines of code, not a brain cell. A single neuron takes a handful of numbers, multiplies each one by something, adds them up, and spits one number out the other end. By itself, useless. The interesting stuff happens when you wire thousands or millions of these together into layers, where each neuron's output becomes input for the next layer.

What makes the network actually do anything is the weights  -  those "multiply each one by something" numbers. Every connection between two neurons has a weight, and those weights are what get adjusted during training. You start with the weights set randomly, so the network's first guesses are garbage. You feed it an example with a known correct answer, measure how wrong its guess was, and nudge every weight slightly in the direction that would have made it a little less wrong. Then you do that again. And again. After a few hundred million rounds, the weights settle into a configuration where the network actually gets things right  -  and somehow, that's all there is to "learning."

Pretty much every AI tool you've used in the last decade runs on this. ChatGPT, your phone's face unlock, the YouTube recommendation feed, the autocorrect on your keyboard. The architectures vary  -  transformers for language, convolutional networks for images, diffusion models for picture generation  -  and the scale can get absurd (trillion-weight models exist now). But underneath, it's still the same setup: layers of small math units, weighted connections between them, and a training process that quietly shoves those weights around until the whole thing works.
terms.analogy: Picture a really long line of office interns sitting at desks, passing notes down the line. Each intern has exactly one rule they follow  -  "if the number on the note is above ten, double it and pass it on," that kind of thing. None of them knows what the line as a whole is doing; they just follow their one rule. But somehow, by the time the note gets to the end of the line, it says "cat"  -  and an image really had been handed to the first person. The trick is that the intern at the start is staring at raw pixel values, the interns in the middle each notice slightly more abstract things ("there's an edge here," "this looks like fur"), and the one at the end is just announcing the final answer. Training is the boss walking up and down the line for months, telling each intern which other interns to listen to and which to ignore, until the line reliably produces the right answer.
terms.howItWorks:
A network has three kinds of layers: an input layer that takes the raw data in, one or more hidden layers in the middle, and an output layer that produces the final answer. Each neuron in one layer is connected to neurons in the next layer through weighted links. Inside a single neuron, what happens is straightforward: it takes every incoming number, multiplies each by the weight of the link it came in through, adds them all up, adds a separate adjustable number called a bias, and then passes the total through an activation function  -  basically a curve that decides whether this neuron should fire strongly, weakly, or not at all. Without that curve at the end, the whole network collapses to plain linear math and can't learn anything interesting.

Then there's the actual learning, which works through something called backpropagation. The idea is: you let the network make a prediction, compare it to the correct answer, and compute how badly it screwed up (the "loss"). Then you walk that error backwards through the network, figuring out how much each individual weight contributed to it, and you nudge each weight a tiny bit in the direction that would have reduced the error. The "tiny bit" is set by the learning rate  -  too big and the network never settles, too small and it takes forever. You repeat the whole thing across millions of training examples, and eventually the weights stop changing much because the network has more or less figured out the problem.
terms.inPractice:
In practice, you don't build a neural network from scratch every time  -  you use frameworks like PyTorch or TensorFlow that handle the math and the training loop for you. The interesting decisions are at a higher level: how many layers, how wide they should be, what kind of architecture to pick. Image work almost always uses convolutional layers. Anything involving sequences (text, audio, time-series) used to use recurrent networks like RNNs and LSTMs, but those got mostly replaced by transformers a few years back. Image generation models like Stable Diffusion are a different beast called diffusion models. The naming is a mess and the field moves fast, so even practitioners struggle to keep up.

The thing nobody quite gets used to is how expensive and how opaque modern networks are. Training a large one can cost millions of dollars in compute, and once it's done, even the team that trained it can't tell you exactly why it makes a specific decision on a specific input. You can pop the hood, look at activations, run interpretability tools  -  but at the end of the day, you've got billions of numbers in there and very little theory about what any individual number is really doing. This is the trade-off the whole industry has quietly accepted: we can build things that work shockingly well, but we've largely given up on understanding them from the inside.
keyPoints: A neural network is just layered math  -  each neuron takes numbers in, multiplies by weights, adds them up, passes the result on
All the "intelligence" lives in the weights; training is the slow process of adjusting them based on how wrong each guess was
The same basic recipe runs almost everything: ChatGPT, face unlock, recommendation feeds, image generation
Modern networks work shockingly well, but nobody can fully explain why a trained network makes any particular decision
