categories.name: LLMs
categories.description: Large Language Models  -  the technology behind ChatGPT and friends.

terms.name: Large Language Model (LLM)
terms.tagline: A neural network trained on vast text data that can understand and generate human-like text
terms.plainEnglish:
A Large Language Model (LLM) is a type of artificial intelligence trained on enormous amounts of text - billions of web pages, books, articles, and code repositories. It learns patterns in language: how words relate to each other, how sentences are structured, and even how reasoning works. Once trained, it can generate new text, answer questions, summarize documents, write code, translate languages, and much more.

LLMs are what power tools like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. They're called "large" because they have billions (sometimes trillions) of internal parameters and are trained on massive datasets. The size is what gives them their surprising capabilities - including skills that emerge only at scale, like basic reasoning, translation, and following instructions they were never explicitly taught.

Despite how intelligent they seem, LLMs don't actually "think" or "understand" in the human sense. They are extremely sophisticated next-token predictors: given a sequence of words, they predict which word should come next, over and over again. The magic is that this simple mechanism, scaled up enormously, produces remarkably coherent and useful text.
terms.analogy: An LLM is like a librarian who has read every book in existence but has no actual experiences in the world. Ask them about anything they've read, and they can give you a brilliant summary, connect ideas across books, and even invent plausible new stories in the style of your favourite author. But they've never tasted food, seen a sunset, or felt an emotion. They know about the world through text alone - and that text is both their knowledge and their limitation.
terms.howItWorks: LLMs are built on the transformer architecture, specifically the decoder-only variant. Text is first broken into tokens (small chunks of characters), then converted to numerical representations called embeddings. These pass through dozens or hundreds of transformer layers, each using a mechanism called self-attention to weigh the importance of every other token when processing each token. The model is trained using next token prediction: it reads text left-to-right and predicts the next token at every position. The error between its prediction and the actual text is used to adjust its internal parameters via backpropagation. After seeing billions of such examples, the model develops a rich internal representation of language patterns, facts, and even reasoning chains. Training an LLM is enormously expensive - millions of GPU-hours and tens of millions of dollars. But running it (called inference) is relatively cheap: each prediction is just a single forward pass through the network. This is why companies invest heavily in training once, then sell access to the trained model.
terms.inPractice:
LLMs have become the default interface for AI. Developers access them through APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta) and integrate them into applications for chat, code generation, content creation, data analysis, and automation. Open-source LLMs like Llama, Mistral, and Qwen allow developers to run models on their own infrastructure.

Choosing an LLM involves trade-offs: larger models are more capable but slower and more expensive; smaller models are faster and cheaper but less capable. The right choice depends on your task, latency requirements, and budget. Most production applications use a mix of models - a small fast one for simple tasks and a large powerful one for complex reasoning.
keyPoints: A neural network trained on vast text data to understand and generate human-like text
Built on the decoder-only transformer architecture with self-attention
Training costs $10M-$100M+; inference is relatively cheap
Powers ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini -- the default AI interface of 2025-2026

terms.name: GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)
terms.tagline: A family of decoder-only LLMs developed by OpenAI, starting a revolution in generative AI
terms.note:
⚡ As of 2026, the latest GPT model is GPT-5.5, powering the current ChatGPT.
Each generation pushes capability, efficiency, and alignment further.
terms.plainEnglish: GPT stands for "Generative Pre-trained Transformer," a family of large language models created by OpenAI. The name breaks down into three parts: Generative (it produces new text), Pre-trained (it's trained on vast text data before any fine-tuning), and Transformer (the neural network architecture it's built on). GPT-3, released in 2020, was a watershed moment - it showed that scaling up a language model to 175 billion parameters led to abilities that simply didn't exist in smaller models, like few-shot learning and basic reasoning. GPT-3.5 (a refined version) was the model behind the original ChatGPT when it launched in November 2022, which became the fastest-growing consumer application in history. GPT-4 added multimodal capabilities (understanding images) and significantly improved reasoning. Each generation of GPT has pushed the frontier of what LLMs can do, and the series has become the de facto benchmark for the entire field. While GPT is OpenAI's proprietary technology, its architecture - the decoder-only transformer with causal attention - has been widely adopted and replicated in open-source models like Llama, Mistral, and others. The "GPT-style" architecture is now the dominant paradigm in LLMs.
terms.analogy: GPT models are like a chef who learned to cook by reading every recipe ever written. Give them the first few ingredients of any dish, and they can finish it with startling accuracy. Sometimes they invent new recipes that are brilliant; other times they add ingredients that don't exist or make dishes that sound delicious but would be inedible. Their skill comes purely from the volume of recipes they've studied, not from ever tasting food.
terms.howItWorks: GPT uses a decoder-only transformer architecture with causal attention - each token can only attend to tokens before it, not after. This unidirectional design is ideal for text generation, where you generate one token at a time left-to-right. The model takes in a sequence of tokens, passes them through multiple transformer layers, and outputs a probability distribution over the next token. The "pre-training" phase uses next token prediction on a massive corpus of internet text. After pre-training, GPT models undergo instruction tuning and RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) to align them with user intent - making them helpful, harmless, and honest. This multi-stage training pipeline is what separates a raw language model from a useful assistant.
terms.inPractice:
GPT models are accessed through OpenAI's API (gpt-4o, gpt-4o-mini, o1, o3) or through consumer products like ChatGPT. Developers use the API to build chatbots, code assistants, content generators, summarization tools, and more. The release of GPT-3's API in 2020 sparked an entire ecosystem of AI-powered applications.

GPT has also driven significant open-source innovation. The architecture has been replicated in numerous open models, and OpenAI's research papers (especially on scaling laws, RLHF, and instruction tuning) have shaped the entire field. While GPT itself remains closed, its influence on AI development is immeasurable.
keyPoints: Family of LLMs by OpenAI: Generative Pre-trained Transformer
GPT-3 (175B params, 2020) showed scaling unlocks emergent abilities
ChatGPT launched November 2022 -- fastest-growing consumer app in history
Architecture: decoder-only transformer with causal attention

terms.name: Foundation Model
terms.tagline: A large AI model trained on broad data that serves as a base for many downstream tasks
terms.plainEnglish: A foundation model is a large AI model trained on broad, general-purpose data that can be adapted to a wide range of specific tasks. The term was popularized by the Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models (CRFM) in 2021 to describe a paradigm shift: instead of training separate models for every task, you train one massive model on everything and adapt it for specific uses. Foundation models are defined by three properties: breadth (trained on diverse data covering many domains), scale (billions of parameters trained on trillions of tokens), and adaptability (they can be fine-tuned, prompted, or otherwise specialized for specific tasks). GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, and Llama are all foundation models. This paradigm represents a fundamental shift in AI development. Prior to foundation models, most AI systems were built from scratch for each task. Now the standard approach is: start with a foundation model, then specialize. This dramatically reduces the cost and expertise needed to build AI applications.
terms.analogy: A foundation model is like a university degree. You spend years getting a broad education - math, literature, history, science - without knowing exactly what job you'll end up in. Later, when you start a specific career, you build on that broad foundation with specialized on-the-job training. The broad education makes the specialization possible and much faster than starting from zero.
terms.howItWorks: Foundation models are typically trained in two phases. The first phase is pre-training on massive unlabeled data using self-supervised learning (usually next-token prediction for language models). This phase is enormously expensive - requiring thousands of GPUs running for weeks or months - but produces a model with broad knowledge. The second phase is adaptation: fine-tuning the pre-trained model on specific tasks, often with much smaller datasets and compute budgets. The key insight behind foundation models is transfer learning: the knowledge learned during pre-training transfers to downstream tasks, meaning you need far less task-specific data than training from scratch. This is what makes modern AI practical - a single foundation model can be adapted for thousands of different applications by different teams.
terms.inPractice:
Foundation models are the backbone of the modern AI industry. Companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Meta train foundation models and offer access through APIs. Developers then fine-tune or prompt these models for specific use cases: customer support, code generation, legal document analysis, medical diagnosis assistance, and more.

The foundation model ecosystem includes both proprietary models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) and open-weight models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen). Open models allow organizations to run foundation models on their own infrastructure, which is important for privacy, compliance, and cost control at scale.
keyPoints: A large model trained on broad data that can be adapted to many tasks
Term coined by Stanford CRFM in 2021
Trained in two phases: expensive pre-training + cheap adaptation/fine-tuning
Paradigm shift: one base model powers thousands of applications

terms.name: Generative AI
terms.tagline: AI systems that create new content - text, images, code, audio, or video - rather than just analyzing data
terms.plainEnglish: Generative AI refers to artificial intelligence that can produce new content rather than just classify or analyze existing data. While previous AI systems could tell you whether an email was spam or recommend a movie, generative AI can write the email, create the movie poster, compose the soundtrack, and write the code for the streaming app. Generative AI includes several subfields: text generation (LLMs like GPT and Claude), image generation (DALL-E, Midjourney, Stable Diffusion), code generation (GitHub Copilot, Cursor), music generation, video generation, and even 3D model creation. The common thread is that these models learn the distribution of their training data and then sample from it to create novel outputs. The term has become a buzzword, but it represents a genuine technological shift. Generative AI is predicted to add trillions of dollars to the global economy by automating creative and knowledge work. It's also raised important questions about copyright, authenticity, and the nature of creativity itself.
terms.analogy: Generative AI is like a master forger who can paint in the style of any artist after studying enough of their work. Show them enough Van Gogh paintings, and they can create a new Van Gogh that never existed - capturing the brushstrokes, colour palette, and emotional tone. They're not "inspired" the way a human artist is, but the output can be indistinguishable from the real thing.
terms.howItWorks: Different generative AI architectures use different techniques. For text, transformer-based LLMs predict next tokens auto-regressively. For images, diffusion models learn to reverse a process that gradually adds noise to images - starting from pure noise and iteratively refining it into a coherent picture matching a text description. For code, models are trained on source code and learn to complete or generate functions based on context. What unifies these approaches is that they all model probability distributions over their respective data types. The model learns what patterns are likely in the training data (what words tend to follow other words, what pixels form a cat face, what code implements a sorting algorithm) and then samples from that learned distribution to create new content.
terms.inPractice: Generative AI is reshaping industries. In software development, GitHub Copilot and similar tools generate code from natural language descriptions, boosting developer productivity by 30-50% on many tasks. In marketing and design, tools like Midjourney and Canva's AI features generate visuals from text prompts. In media, AI writes draft articles, creates video summaries, and generates voiceovers. However, generative AI has limitations: it can hallucinate (make up plausible-sounding falsehoods), produce biased or harmful content, and its outputs aren't always reliable. Responsible use requires human oversight, validation, and clear disclosure that content is AI-generated.
keyPoints: Creates new content (text, images, code, audio, video) rather than just analysing
Text: transformer LLMs; Images: diffusion models; Code: code-trained LMs
Predicted to add trillions to the global economy
Limitations: hallucinations, bias, and need for human oversight

terms.name: Tokens
terms.tagline: The atomic units of text that LLMs process - words, subwords, or characters converted to numerical IDs
terms.plainEnglish: Tokens are the fundamental building blocks that language models work with. Before any text enters an LLM, it passes through a tokenizer that breaks the text into tokens - fragments that might be whole words, parts of words, punctuation marks, or even spaces. The sequence "Hello, world!" might become ["Hello", ",", " world", "!"] - four tokens, each with a unique numerical ID. Tokenization is necessary because neural networks can't process raw text - they can only process numbers. The tokenizer converts text into a sequence of integer IDs, each mapping to a token in the model's vocabulary (typically 32,000 to 128,000 tokens). The model processes these IDs through its layers and generates token IDs as output, which are then decoded back into readable text. Tokens matter because they determine cost, speed, and capability. Most AI APIs charge per token - both input and output. Longer texts cost more. Models also have a context window measured in tokens - the maximum they can process at once - so understanding tokens helps you work within these limits.
terms.analogy: Tokens are like LEGO bricks. When you build with LEGO, you don't use one giant custom piece for every shape - you use many small standard-sized bricks that click together. The tokenizer is like a machine that breaks text into those standard bricks. The LLM then works with those bricks to understand and generate meaning. Different tokenizers use differently-shaped bricks, which is why the same text can be a different number of tokens in different models.
terms.howItWorks: Most modern LLMs use Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) or similar subword tokenization algorithms. The tokenizer is built by analyzing a large corpus and finding the most efficient way to split text into a fixed-size vocabulary. Common words become single tokens ("the", "and", "is"), while rare or long words get split into multiple subword pieces ("unbelievable" might become ["un", "believable"]). On average, one English word equals about 1.3 tokens, but this varies enormously. Short common words might be 1 token; technical terms might be 3-5 tokens. Different languages tokenize differently - languages with non-Latin scripts often need more tokens per word. Specialized content like code or math also has different tokenization patterns. Some models (like Gemini) use character-level or byte-level tokenization for more efficient encoding of mixed content.
terms.inPractice:
Every interaction with an LLM is priced in tokens. GPT-4o might cost $2.50 per million input tokens and $10 per million output tokens. A typical page of text (~500 words) is roughly 650 tokens. A full novel (~100,000 words) is about 130,000 tokens. Understanding tokens helps you estimate costs, stay within context limits, and structure prompts efficiently.

Token limits affect your application design. If your context window is 128K tokens but your documents total 200K tokens, you need strategies like chunking, summarization, or retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to fit within limits. Some models offer "prompt caching" that discounts tokens that are reused across requests - a key optimization for production applications.
keyPoints: Tokens are the atomic text units LLMs process -- words, subwords, or characters
Tokenized via Byte-Pair Encoding into numerical IDs
Typical vocabulary: 32K-128K tokens
Cost and context windows are measured in tokens -- ~0.75 English words per token

terms.name: Completion
terms.tagline: The text an LLM generates as a continuation of a given prompt or input
terms.plainEnglish: A completion is the text produced by a language model in response to an input. You give the model a prompt, and it generates a completion - one token at a time, each new token conditioned on everything that came before. In a chat interface, your question is the prompt and the AI's answer is the completion. In a code assistant, your code and comment are the prompt and the generated code is the completion. Completions can range from a single word to thousands of tokens. The quality depends on the model's training, the prompt's clarity, and the sampling parameters used during generation (temperature, top-k, top-p). Different parameters produce wildly different completions from the same prompt - from factual and deterministic to creative and surprising. In chat models, completions are often called "responses" or "assistant messages," but the underlying mechanism is identical to text completion: the model generates tokens auto-regressively until it reaches a stop condition (a stop sequence, a max token limit, or a special end-of-text token).
terms.analogy: A completion is like the second half of a "fill in the blank" exercise in school. The teacher writes a partial sentence, and you figure out what comes next. The model does the same thing, one word at a time - reading what came before and predicting what logically follows. Unlike a student, the model doesn't "understand" the sentence - it just has a statistical model of what words tend to follow other words.
terms.howItWorks: During completion, the model runs in auto-regressive mode. It starts with the input tokens (the prompt), runs them through the neural network, and gets a probability distribution over all possible next tokens. It picks one token using the configured sampling strategy - this becomes the first token of the completion. Then it appends that token to the input and runs the network again to predict the next token. This loops until a stop condition is met. The output of each forward pass is a logit vector - raw scores for every token in the vocabulary. These are converted to probabilities via softmax, optionally adjusted by temperature (which flattens or sharpens the distribution), and then filtered by techniques like top-k or top-p sampling. The final token is either greedily chosen (highest probability) or randomly sampled from the filtered distribution.
terms.inPractice: Completions are what you're paying for in AI APIs - prices are usually split between input tokens (the prompt) and output tokens (the completion). Optimizing your prompts to produce shorter completions can significantly reduce costs. Some APIs support streaming, where tokens are delivered one-by-one as they're generated, giving a real-time typing effect rather than waiting for the full response. Completion parameters are tuneable per request. You can set temperature, top-k, top-p, max tokens, and stop sequences on each API call. The same prompt with different parameters generates completely different completions - from very predictable (low temperature) to very creative (high temperature). Finding the right parameters for your use case is part of prompt engineering.
keyPoints: The text an LLM generates in response to a prompt, one token at a time
Generation is auto-regressive: each token depends on all previous ones
Controlled by sampling parameters: temperature, top-K, top-P
APIs price input and output tokens separately

facts.label: Stop conditions
facts.value: Stop sequence, max tokens, or EOS token

terms.name: Temperature
terms.tagline: A sampling parameter controlling randomness - higher values produce more creative outputs, lower values more deterministic ones
terms.plainEnglish: Temperature is a parameter that controls how random or deterministic a language model's output is. It works by scaling the logits (raw prediction scores) before converting them to probabilities. Low temperatures (close to 0) make the model pick the most likely token almost every time - producing focused, predictable, and often repetitive text. High temperatures (1.0 and above) flatten the probability distribution, giving less likely tokens a better chance - producing more diverse, creative, and sometimes surprising outputs. Temperature is often described as the "creativity dial." A temperature of 0 makes the model deterministic - the same prompt always produces the same output (equivalent to greedy decoding). A temperature of 0.7 is a common default for creative tasks like story writing or brainstorming. A temperature of 1.5 or above tends to produce increasingly random and often nonsensical text. The right temperature depends entirely on your use case. For factual tasks (summarization, data extraction, translation), use low temperature (0.0-0.2). For creative tasks (story generation, marketing copy, idea generation), use higher temperature (0.7-1.0). For code generation, many developers use 0.0-0.3 to avoid syntax errors.
terms.analogy: Temperature is like the "variety dial" on a jukebox. At low temperature, the jukebox plays the most popular song every time. Every request gets "Bohemian Rhapsody." At medium temperature, it still plays hits but occasionally throws in a deep cut or a B-side. At high temperature, you might get anything - a polka version of a rap song, a 20-minute prog rock epic, or just static. The jukebox has no taste - it just follows its programming - but the dial changes how predictable the outcome is.
terms.howItWorks: The temperature parameter is applied during the sampling step, after the model produces logits but before selecting a token. Mathematically, the logits are divided by the temperature value before softmax: softmax(logits / T). When T is small (e.g., 0.1), the logits are amplified, making small differences much larger - the highest-probability token dominates. When T is large (e.g., 1.5), the logits are compressed, making the distribution more uniform. At T = 1.0, the softmax output matches the model's raw probability estimates. At T = 0, the math changes - instead of dividing by zero, the model always picks the token with the highest logit (greedy decoding). At T approaching infinity, all tokens become equally likely, producing pure random text. Temperature is usually used in combination with top-k or top-p sampling, which further constrain the candidate pool before sampling.
terms.inPractice: Temperature is one of the most commonly tuned generation parameters. Most AI APIs accept it as a float between 0 and 2. A common setup for production applications uses temperature 0 for reliability (customer service responses, data extraction) and temperature 0.7-0.9 for creative tasks (content generation, brainstorming). Some advanced techniques use dynamic temperature - adjusting the temperature during generation (higher at the start for creativity, lower toward the end for coherence). You can also use temperature annealing: starting with high temperature to explore possibilities and lowering it to settle on a consistent output. Most applications, however, use a single fixed temperature per request.
keyPoints: Controls randomness of LLM output -- lower is more deterministic, higher is more creative
Applied by dividing logits by T before softmax
Range typically 0.0-2.0; 0.0 = greedy decoding, 0.7 = common creative default
Use low temp for factual tasks, high temp for creative tasks

terms.name: Top-K Sampling
terms.tagline: A sampling strategy that restricts token selection to the K most likely next tokens
terms.plainEnglish:
Top-K sampling is a technique used during text generation to improve quality by limiting which tokens the model can choose from. Instead of considering every word in the model's entire vocabulary (which might be 100,000+ tokens), the model only considers the K most likely ones. It then re-normalizes the probabilities of just those K tokens and samples from that restricted set.

The intuition is simple: at any given point, most tokens in the vocabulary are terrible continuations. "The capital of France is" should not be followed by "banana" or "giraffe." Top-K eliminates these obviously bad choices automatically. The model picks from only the plausible continuations, which makes the output more coherent while still allowing variety among the top candidates.

Common values for K range from 10 to 100. Lower values (e.g., K=10) produce more focused output; higher values (e.g., K=100) allow more variety. The limitation is that K is fixed - it doesn't adapt to the shape of the probability distribution. When the model is very confident (one token dominates), K=50 still includes 49 unlikely tokens unnecessarily. When the model is uncertain (many tokens have similar probability), K=50 might exclude some reasonable candidates. This is why top-P sampling is often preferred as an adaptive alternative.
terms.analogy: Top-K is like a cooking competition where only the top K finalists get to present their dishes. No matter how many contestants entered, only the best K are judged. If K=3, you taste the top 3 dishes. If all 3 are excellent, you still pick from just those 3 - even if the 4th place dish was also excellent. If the top 3 are terrible, you're stuck choosing from them because K is fixed. The rule is simple but inflexible.
terms.howItWorks: After the model generates logits for all tokens in its vocabulary, top-K selects the K tokens with the highest logits (or probabilities). All other tokens are discarded - their probabilities set to zero. The probabilities of the remaining K tokens are then re-normalized to sum to 1, and the next token is sampled from this filtered distribution. Top-K is often combined with temperature. First, temperature is applied to the logits (dividing by T), then top-K filters to the top K tokens, then softmax converts to probabilities, then the final token is sampled. The order matters: temperature before top-K means the logit scaling affects which tokens make the top-K cutoff.
terms.inPractice:
Top-K is available as a parameter in most LLM APIs alongside temperature and top-P. Many developers use top-K as a secondary filter - applying top-P first and then top-K as an additional constraint, or vice versa. A common configuration is temperature 0.7 with top-K 40 and top-P 0.9.

Top-K alone can be brittle because the optimal K varies by task and model. A K that works well for creative writing might be too restrictive for factual Q&A or too permissive for code generation. Most modern applications use top-P (nucleus sampling) as the primary sampling constraint and top-K as a hard limit to prevent very low-probability tokens in edge cases.
keyPoints: Restricts token selection to the K most likely candidates at each step
K is fixed regardless of the probability distribution shape
Common K values: 10-100
Often combined with temperature and top-P for better results

terms.name: Top-P (Nucleus) Sampling
terms.tagline: A sampling strategy that selects from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds threshold P
terms.plainEnglish: Top-P sampling, also called nucleus sampling, is an adaptive alternative to top-K that chooses a different number of candidate tokens depending on the shape of the probability distribution. Instead of fixing the number of candidates, you set a probability threshold P (e.g., 0.9). The model then selects the smallest set of tokens whose combined probability reaches that threshold - this set is called the "nucleus." The advantage over top-K is adaptivity. When the model is confident (a few tokens dominate), the nucleus is small - you only consider the most likely continuations. When the model is uncertain (many tokens have similar probability), the nucleus expands to include more candidates. This matches how humans think about ambiguous situations: when only one continuation makes sense, you don't waste time on alternatives; when many are plausible, you consider more options. Typical P values range from 0.8 to 0.95. A P of 0.9 means the model will consider enough tokens to cover 90% of the probability mass. Higher P values (e.g., 0.99) include almost all tokens and approach the full distribution. Lower values (e.g., 0.8) are more restrictive. Top-P produces more natural, varied text than top-K in many cases and is widely used in production LLM applications.
terms.analogy: Top-P is like inviting people to a party until you reach your capacity limit. If the first 5 VIPs fill your venue's capacity, you stop at 5. If you need 100 people to fill it, you invite 100. Unlike top-K which always invites exactly K people (whether or not that fills the room), top-P adapts - it invites whatever number reaches the desired fill level. The "P" is your capacity; the guest list size varies.
terms.howItWorks: The algorithm is straightforward: (1) sort all tokens by probability descending, (2) start adding tokens from highest to lowest probability, (3) stop when the cumulative probability exceeds the threshold P, (4) discard all unselected tokens, (5) re-normalize the probabilities of selected tokens to sum to 1, (6) sample from this filtered distribution. The threshold P directly controls the trade-off between quality and diversity. Lower P values produce more focused, predictable text (fewer tokens in the nucleus). Higher P values allow more variety but risk including low-quality continuations. At P = 1.0, top-P has no effect - all tokens are included. Top-P is often used together with temperature and sometimes top-K. A common pipeline: apply temperature to logits, then apply top-P (or top-K as a hard cutoff), then sample. Many models recommend temperature 1.0 with top-P 0.9-0.95 as a starting point.
terms.inPractice:
Top-P is widely supported in LLM APIs. OpenAI recommends using either temperature or top-P but not both as primary controls, though many practitioners combine them. Anthropic's API documentation suggests top-P as a useful secondary control alongside temperature.

Top-P excels in creative and open-ended generation tasks like story writing, dialogue generation, and brainstorming where multiple valid continuations exist. For highly constrained tasks like code generation or factual Q&A, lower top-P values (0.8-0.9) or greedy decoding (temperature 0) often work better. The flexibility of top-P makes it the default sampling method in many modern LLM inference frameworks.
keyPoints: Selects from tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds threshold P
Adaptive: few candidates when confident, many when uncertain
Typical P range: 0.7-0.95
Also called nucleus sampling -- widely used in production LLMs

terms.name: Hallucination
terms.tagline: When an LLM generates factually incorrect or nonsensical information while presenting it with confidence
terms.plainEnglish: Hallucination is the term for when an AI model produces information that sounds plausible but is factually wrong, completely made up, or nonsensical. The model doesn't "lie" - it has no intent to deceive - but it confidently states incorrect facts as if they were true. A model might invent a historical event, cite a nonexistent research paper, or describe a person who never existed. Hallucinations happen because LLMs are next-token predictors, not factual databases. They're optimized to generate text that sounds convincing and follows linguistic patterns, not to verify truth. When the model encounters a gap in its knowledge, it doesn't say "I don't know" - it generates whatever continuation is most probable based on its training, which may be entirely fabricated. There are two main types of hallucination. Closed-book hallucinations occur when the model doesn't know the answer and makes something up. Input-conflict hallucinations occur when the model contradicts the user's provided context or instructions. Both are significant challenges in deploying LLMs for factual tasks.
terms.analogy: Hallucination is like a confident friend who has opinions about everything but never admits uncertainty. Ask them about Impressionist painters and they'll give you a brilliant lecture. Ask them about quantum chromodynamics and they'll give you an equally confident answer - which might be complete nonsense. Their confidence is the same either way. The problem isn't that they occasionally make mistakes; it's that you can't tell when they're right and when they're inventing.
terms.howItWorks: LLMs are trained to minimize loss on next-token prediction across their training corpus. This doesn't include any explicit training on truthfulness or fact-checking. A model trained on internet data learns to mimic the patterns of confident, authoritative-sounding text - even when the specific "knowledge" it's drawing on is incorrect, outdated, or contradictory. During generation, the model samples from its probability distribution over tokens. If the correct answer to a question isn't well-represented in its training data, the most probable continuation might be a fabricated but plausible-sounding alternative. The model has no internal "I don't know" signal - it generates tokens based purely on statistical patterns, not self-awareness. Mitigation techniques include RAG (retrieval-augmented generation - giving the model external documents to ground its answers), RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback, which penalizes factual errors during fine-tuning), and prompt engineering (instructions like "only answer if you're certain" or "say you don't know"). However, no technique eliminates hallucinations entirely.
terms.inPractice: Hallucination is one of the biggest challenges in deploying LLMs for real-world applications. In customer service, a hallucinated answer can mislead users. In legal or medical contexts, it can cause serious harm. In code generation, hallucinated APIs or deprecated functions can cause bugs. Any production application must implement safeguards. The standard approach to mitigating hallucinations is grounding - providing the model with trusted source documents and instructing it to base its answers only on those sources. This is the core idea behind RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation). Other techniques include chain-of-thought prompting (which reduces hallucinations by making the model reason step by step), multiple responses with voting, and citation requirements (forcing the model to cite its sources).
keyPoints: Model confidently generates false or nonsensical information
Root cause: models optimise for plausibility, not truth
Two main types: closed-book (doesn't know) and input-conflict (contradicts context)
Primary mitigation: RAG (grounding responses in trusted sources)

facts.label: Root cause
facts.value: Models optimize for plausibility, not truth

terms.name: Next Token Prediction
terms.tagline: The core training objective for most LLMs - predicting the next token in a sequence given all previous ones
terms.plainEnglish: Next token prediction is the fundamental training objective that powers virtually every modern LLM. The idea is simple: given a sequence of tokens, predict the next one. The model reads "The cat sat on the" and learns to predict "mat." It does this at every position in every sequence, learning not just grammar and vocabulary but also facts, reasoning patterns, stylistic conventions, and even world knowledge. This is a self-supervised learning objective - it requires no human labels. The input text itself provides both the input (all tokens up to position N) and the label (the token at position N+1). This is what allows LLMs to be trained on massive unlabeled text corpora. Every sentence on the internet becomes countless training examples: for a sentence of 100 tokens, there are 99 prediction tasks. The simplicity and scalability of next-token prediction is what enabled the rapid scaling of LLMs from millions to trillions of parameters. It's also surprisingly powerful - this single objective produces models capable of translation, reasoning, coding, and conversation, even though they were never explicitly trained on those tasks.
terms.analogy: Next token prediction is like learning a language by doing millions of fill-in-the-blank exercises. You never study grammar rules, memorize vocabulary lists, or learn facts about the world. You just get increasingly good at guessing the next word: "The Eiffel Tower is in ___" → "Paris." "The chemical symbol for gold is ___" → "Au." "To reverse a linked list, you ___" → "iterate." After enough examples, you're an expert at predicting what comes next - and by extension, you've absorbed an enormous amount of knowledge.
terms.howItWorks: During training, the model processes sequences of tokens and is trained to predict each token given only the tokens before it. A causal attention mask ensures the model can't peek at future tokens. The loss is cross-entropy between the model's predicted probability distribution and the actual next token. The model's parameters are updated via backpropagation to minimize this loss across billions of examples. The training data is typically organized into batches of sequences. For each sequence, the model simultaneously predicts every next token position - this is efficient because all predictions within a sequence can be computed in a single forward pass thanks to the causal mask. One training batch might contain millions of prediction tasks across thousands of sequences. The magic is that next-token prediction implicitly teaches the model much more than just language patterns. To accurately predict the next token, the model must learn factual knowledge ("The capital of France is ___"), reasoning chains ("If A > B and B > C, then A ___ C"), and even long-range dependencies ("In chapter 1, the detective noticed a blue handkerchief. In chapter 15, the clue that solves the case is the ___").
terms.inPractice:
Next token prediction is the standard pre-training objective for all major LLMs - GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Mistral, and others. The pre-training phase is the most computationally expensive part of creating an LLM, consuming thousands of GPUs for weeks. After pre-training, models are typically further refined with instruction tuning and RLHF.

Some newer models explore additional objectives or modifications (like multi-token prediction, where the model predicts several future tokens at once), but next token prediction remains the dominant approach. Its combination of simplicity, scalability, and effectiveness has made it the foundation of modern language AI.
keyPoints: Core training objective for LLMs: predict the next token given all previous ones
Self-supervised -- no human labels needed; text creates its own training examples
This single objective produces reasoning, translation, and coding abilities
The simplicity and scalability of this enabled modern LLMs

terms.name: Causal Language Model
terms.tagline: A decoder-only language model where each token can only attend to previous tokens (unidirectional)
terms.plainEnglish: A causal language model is a type of neural network architecture where the attention mechanism is restricted so that each token can only "look at" tokens that came before it. This creates a strictly unidirectional, left-to-right flow of information. Causal LMs are also called decoder-only models because they use only the decoder stack of the original transformer architecture. This design is essential for text generation: when generating text, you can't look at tokens that haven't been written yet. The causal mask ensures training and inference are consistent - the model never has access to information it wouldn't have during generation. GPT, Claude, Llama, Mistral, and most modern LLMs are causal language models. The alternative is a bidirectional model like BERT, which uses an encoder-only architecture where each token can attend to all other tokens in both directions. Bidirectional models are better for understanding tasks (classification, sentiment analysis) but can't generate text. Causal models excel at generation, which is why they became the dominant architecture for LLMs.
terms.analogy: A causal LM reads like a person reading a book one word at a time. They know what came before, but they can't peek at the next page. They make predictions about what's coming next based only on what they've already read. A bidirectional model, in contrast, is like someone who can scan the entire page at once, seeing every word simultaneously - great for understanding the full page, but impossible to use for writing because they'd always need to see the end before they started.
terms.howItWorks: The key mechanism is the causal attention mask - a triangular matrix applied during the attention computation. For a sequence of N tokens, the attention mask ensures that token at position i can only attend to tokens at positions ≤ i. All future tokens are masked out (their attention weights set to -infinity before softmax, making their contribution zero). During training, the causal mask enables efficient parallel computation: all N prediction tasks (predicting token 2 given token 1, predicting token 3 given tokens 1-2, etc.) can be computed in a single forward pass. This dramatically accelerates training compared to generating tokens one at a time. The architecture typically consists of an embedding layer (converting token IDs to vectors), followed by N transformer decoder layers (each containing self-attention with causal masking, feed-forward networks, and layer normalization), and finally a language modeling head (projecting to vocabulary-sized logits).
terms.inPractice: Causal LMs are the default architecture for text generation. When you use ChatGPT, Claude, or any modern chatbot, you're interacting with a causal language model. The architecture supports efficient KV caching - storing the key-value pairs from previous attention computations so that generation doesn't require re-processing the entire sequence for each new token, dramatically speeding up inference. The decoder-only architecture has become so dominant that most LLM research and development focuses on improving it: better attention mechanisms (flash attention, multi-query attention), more efficient architectures (Mamba, RWKV), and larger-scale training. While bidirectional models like BERT are still used for embeddings and classification, causal LMs have largely won the generative AI race.
keyPoints: Decoder-only architecture where each token only attends to previous tokens
Essential for text generation -- can't peek at future tokens
Uses a causal attention mask (triangular matrix)
GPT, Claude, Llama, Mistral -- most modern LLMs are causal LMs

terms.name: Base Model vs Chat Model
terms.tagline: Base models predict next token without special formatting; chat models are fine-tuned for multi-turn instruction following
terms.plainEnglish: Base models and chat models are two versions of the same underlying LLM that serve different purposes. A base model (or pre-trained model) has only been trained on raw text using next token prediction. It can continue text - give it "The capital of France is" and it'll output "Paris" - but it doesn't understand instructions, follow conversational formats, or refuse harmful requests. It's a raw text completion engine. A chat model (or instruct model) starts with a base model and undergoes additional training to make it useful in conversation. This involves instruction tuning (training on datasets of instruction-response pairs) and RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) to align with human preferences. Chat models understand when you're asking a question vs. continuing a sentence, follow complex instructions, maintain context across turns, and refuse inappropriate requests. The distinction matters because each is suited for different tasks. Base models are ideal for text completion, fine-tuning on specialized data, and running as a foundation for custom applications. Chat models are ideal for interactive use, customer-facing chatbots, and any application that requires instruction following.
terms.analogy: A base model is like a brilliant but socially awkward scholar who has memorized every book in the library. Ask them to continue a sentence - they're incredible. Ask them to "explain quantum physics in simple terms for a child" - they might just continue the sentence "explain quantum physics in simple terms for a child is a difficult task" rather than actually doing it. The chat model is that same scholar after charm school: they learned the social rules of conversation, how to follow instructions, and how to be helpful rather than just predictive.
terms.howItWorks: Creating a chat model from a base model is a multi-step process. First, supervised fine-tuning (SFT): the base model is trained on thousands or millions of instruction-response examples, teaching it to follow instructions and engage in dialogue. Then, RLHF: human raters compare model responses and rank them; a reward model is trained to predict human preferences; the language model is then fine-tuned to maximize this reward, aligning it with human values. The chat model also uses a different chat template - a structured format that includes roles (system, user, assistant), special tokens to mark boundaries, and conversation history formatting. The base model doesn't use this template; it just continues raw text. Using the wrong template (or no template) with a chat model produces poor results.
terms.inPractice:
When you use ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, you're using a chat model. When you download Llama or Mistral from Hugging Face, you typically get both versions: the base model (e.g., Llama-3-70B) and the chat model (e.g., Llama-3-70B-Instruct). Developers who want to fine-tune on specialized data usually start with the base model and apply their own instruction tuning. Developers who want out-of-the-box conversation use the chat model.

Some applications use both: a base model for batch processing or text completion tasks, and a chat model for interactive components. The choice affects not just behavior but also API usage - chat models are typically more expensive to run because they've been aligned through additional training stages.
keyPoints: Base models predict the next token; chat models add instruction following
Chat models are base models + supervised fine-tuning + RLHF
Base models are ideal for fine-tuning on specialised data
Chat models are ideal for interactive applications and customer-facing bots

terms.name: Context Window
terms.tagline: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single input, including both prompt and generated output
terms.plainEnglish:
The context window is the maximum number of tokens a language model can handle in a single request. This includes both the input you send (your prompt, documents, conversation history) and the output the model generates. If a model has a 128K context window and your prompt is 100K tokens, the model can generate at most 28K tokens in its response.

Context windows have grown dramatically. Early LLMs like GPT-1 and GPT-2 had context windows of 512-1024 tokens. GPT-3 had 2K-4K. GPT-4 Turbo pushed to 128K. Claude 3 and Gemini handle 200K and 1M tokens respectively. This expansion has unlocked new use cases: analyzing entire codebases, reviewing hundred-page documents, and maintaining coherent conversations over hundreds of messages.

However, larger context windows come with trade-offs. The transformer's attention mechanism scales quadratically (O(n²)) with sequence length - doubling the context quadruples the compute cost. Also, models often struggle to effectively use information in the middle of very long contexts, a phenomenon called "lost in the middle." Having the capacity isn't the same as using it well.
terms.analogy: The context window is like your desk space. A small desk means you can only look at a few papers at once - you have to keep putting things away and taking new ones out. A large desk lets you spread out an entire book, your notes, and reference materials all at once. But having a massive desk doesn't mean you'll perfectly remember everything on it - you still might miss papers in the corners or buried under other documents.
terms.howItWorks: The context window is determined by the model's architecture. In standard transformers, the self-attention mechanism computes pairwise attention between every pair of tokens in the sequence, resulting in O(n²) memory and compute costs. This is the fundamental bottleneck on context window size. Techniques to extend context windows include approximate attention (like sparse or sliding window attention), positional encoding improvements (RoPE, ALiBi), and ring attention or distributed attention across multiple GPUs. Information in the context window isn't equally accessible to the model. Research has shown that models tend to recall information at the beginning and end of their context window better than information in the middle - the "lost in the middle" phenomenon. This has practical implications for prompt design: put the most important information at the start or end of your prompt, not in the middle.
terms.inPractice: Context window size is now a major selling point for LLMs. Longer context windows enable: whole-document analysis (uploading a full PDF or book and asking questions), extended conversations (chatting for hundreds of messages without losing context), large codebase understanding (including entire repositories), and multi-document reasoning (comparing several reports or contracts). In practice, you rarely need a massive context window - many tasks fit in 8K-32K tokens. The trade-off is that models with larger context windows are typically slower and more expensive per token. Choose the smallest context window that fits your use case for optimal cost and performance.
keyPoints: Maximum tokens an LLM can process in one request (input + output combined)
Has grown from 512 tokens (GPT-1) to 1M tokens (Gemini)
Attention scales quadratically -- larger context windows are expensive
Models often 'lose' information in the middle of very long contexts

terms.name: Max Tokens
terms.tagline: The maximum number of tokens an LLM is allowed to generate in a single response
terms.plainEnglish:
Max tokens is a generation parameter that sets an upper limit on how long the model's response can be. Since LLMs generate one token at a time indefinitely (they'd keep going until they hit a stop condition), the max tokens parameter acts as a safety limit - it stops generation when the response reaches this length, even if no stop sequence or end-of-text token has been encountered.

This parameter serves two purposes. First, it prevents runaway generation - a model that gets stuck in a loop or is very verbose won't consume unlimited compute time or money. Second, it lets you control output length: set max tokens to 50 for short answers, 500 for medium responses, or 4000+ for long-form content.

The max tokens value can't exceed the model's context window. Since the context window includes both input and output tokens, a long prompt reduces the available space for generation. For example, with a 128K context window and a 100K token prompt, max tokens can be at most 28K.
terms.analogy: Max tokens is like setting a word count limit on an essay. The teacher says "maximum 500 words." You write until you hit 500 words and then stop - even if you haven't finished your point. You should have planned better. Similarly, if the model's response is cut off because it hit max tokens, you might need to increase the limit or make your prompt shorter to give the model more room.
terms.howItWorks:
During generation, after each token is produced, the model checks two conditions: (1) did the output contain a stop sequence? and (2) has the total output length reached max tokens? If either is true, generation halts. The max tokens check is a simple counter - the model counts every token it generates and stops when the counter reaches the max.

Max tokens interacts with other parameters. A high max tokens value combined with low temperature and no stop sequences might produce very long, repetitive output. A low max tokens value combined with a complex prompt might produce truncated, incomplete responses. Setting max tokens appropriately for your use case is an important part of prompt engineering.

In API usage, you're charged for all tokens generated up to max tokens - even if the model stops earlier due to a stop sequence. This means setting max tokens much higher than needed doesn't cost extra if the model stops naturally, but it does add a safety bound in case of unexpected behavior.
terms.inPractice: In production applications, setting an appropriate max tokens value is a cost-control and UX measure. For a chatbot, you might set max tokens to 1024 (about 750 words) to keep responses concise. For a document summarizer, you might set it to 4096. For code generation, the appropriate value depends on the expected function size. Some APIs distinguish between max_tokens (deprecated in favor of max_completion_tokens in newer specs) and max_output_tokens. The distinction is that max_completion_tokens in reasoning models (like o1) includes "thinking" tokens that the model generates internally before producing its visible answer.
keyPoints: Hard limit on how long the model's response can be
Prevents runaway generation and controls costs
Cannot exceed the context window minus prompt length
Lower values = shorter responses; higher = longer but more expensive

facts.label: Constraint
facts.value: Cannot exceed context window minus prompt

terms.name: Stop Sequence
terms.tagline: A user-defined string or set of strings that triggers the LLM to stop generating further tokens
terms.plainEnglish:
A stop sequence is a specific string or list of strings that tells the model "stop generating as soon as you produce this." When generating text, the model checks after each new token whether the output so far ends with one of the specified stop sequences. If it does, generation halts immediately. This gives you semantic control over when the model stops, unlike max tokens which is a hard numerical limit.

Stop sequences are incredibly useful. Want the model to generate only one sentence? Set stop to ["."] and it stops at the first period (though this is too aggressive - periods in abbreviations will also trigger it). Want it to generate a single paragraph? Stop at ["

"]. Want to prevent it from continuing a conversation? Stop at ["User:", "

"] to halt before it starts role-playing the user.

Multiple stop sequences can be specified simultaneously. The model checks all of them after each token, and the first one matched wins. This makes stop sequences a powerful tool for controlling output format and preventing unwanted model behavior.
terms.analogy: A stop sequence is like telling someone "keep talking until you hear me say 'that's enough'." You don't set a timer (like max tokens) - you give a verbal off-switch. As long as they don't hear those words, they keep going. The moment they say "that's enough," they stop mid-sentence. You can have multiple stop words: also stop if I say "moving on" or "next topic." This gives you natural control over the conversation length.
terms.howItWorks:
After each token is generated, the model checks if the concatenation of the new token with previous output ends with any of the specified stop sequences. This is a simple string matching operation - the model isn't "understanding" meaning, just checking for substring matches at the end of the output.

The stop sequence itself is not included in the output. If the stop sequence is "User:" and the model outputs "The answer is 42. User:", the returned response will be "The answer is 42." This prevents stop sequences from appearing in the final text - they're purely boundary markers.

Stop sequences take priority over max tokens. If the model hits a stop sequence before reaching max tokens, generation stops and the response is returned. If it reaches max tokens first, generation stops and the output might be truncated mid-sentence - which is why setting appropriate stop sequences is preferred over relying solely on max tokens for clean output.
terms.inPractice: Stop sequences are available in most LLM APIs. Common use cases include: preventing the model from generating beyond a single response in a chat format (stop at [" User:", " Assistant:"]), extracting a single item from a list (stop at [" "]), terminating code generation when a function ends (stop at [" } "]), and controlling format in structured outputs. One important consideration: stop sequences are exact string matches, not semantic. A stop sequence of ["."] will trigger on "Dr." and "e.g." and "1.5" - not just at the end of sentences. Careful choice of stop sequences (like [" "] for paragraph boundaries) avoids these issues. Some APIs also support stop token IDs - native tokens from the model's vocabulary - which can be more reliable than string matching.
keyPoints: A string that tells the model to stop generating when it appears in the output
More semantic control than a hard token limit
Multiple stop sequences can be specified -- first match wins
The stop sequence itself is not included in the returned output

facts.label: Detection method
facts.value: Exact string matching at output end

facts.label: Not included in output
facts.value: Stop sequence is trimmed from response

terms.name: Beam Search
terms.tagline: A search algorithm that explores multiple candidate sequences at each generation step, keeping the most promising paths
terms.plainEnglish:
Beam search is a text generation strategy that keeps track of multiple possible sequences (called "beams") simultaneously. Instead of committing to a single best token at each step, it keeps the top B candidates (where B is the beam width or beam size) at every step, expanding each one and then keeping the best B again. At the end, it selects the highest-scoring complete sequence.

This is fundamentally different from greedy decoding, which makes locally optimal choices that might miss better global sequences. Beam search sacrifices some of greedy decoding's simplicity for the ability to explore multiple paths. For example, greedy decoding might generate "The cat sat on the mat" because "sat" is the most likely word after "cat." But beam search might discover that "The cat stretched lazily on" leads to a better overall sentence, even though "stretched" was less likely than "sat" at that step.

The beam width controls the trade-off between exploration and computation. A beam width of 1 is equivalent to greedy decoding. A beam width of 4-10 is common for tasks like translation or summarization. Larger beam widths explore more alternatives but are proportionally slower (O(B × V) per step) and require more memory.
terms.analogy: Beam search is like a hiking group exploring a mountain with multiple paths to the summit. Instead of committing to one path at every fork (greedy), the group sends small teams down the most promising paths. At the next fork, each team splits again. After each leg, the least promising teams are recalled, and resources are focused on the most promising routes. By exploring multiple paths simultaneously, the group is more likely to find the best overall route to the summit - even if the first step down a winning path was initially less promising.
terms.howItWorks: At each generation step, beam search maintains B candidate sequences. For each of the B candidates, it considers all V tokens in the vocabulary, creating B × V candidate extensions. It scores each extended sequence (typically using log probability - the sum of log probabilities of all tokens in the sequence). It then selects the B best-scoring extensions to continue. After generation is complete (typically determined by max tokens or stop sequences), the single best sequence is returned. The scoring function is important. If you just sum log probabilities, longer sequences tend to have lower (more negative) scores, biasing toward shorter outputs. To compensate, length normalization is often applied - dividing the score by the sequence length or a function of it. This encourages the model to favor reasonable-length completions. Beam search tends to produce more consistent, higher-quality output than greedy decoding for tasks like machine translation and text summarization. However, it can lead to repetitive or generic text because it favors high-probability sequences. For creative tasks where diversity is desired, sampling methods (temperature, top-K, top-P) are preferred.
terms.inPractice:
Beam search is widely used in machine translation, where the goal is to find the single best translation and quality is paramount. Google Translate has historically used beam search. It's also common in speech recognition and text summarization. In these settings, beam width 4-10 is typical.

Beam search is less common in conversational AI and creative writing, where sampling methods produce more natural, diverse text. It's also falling out of favor as newer models are trained to produce better output with simpler decoding strategies. Many modern inference frameworks support beam search but default to sampling. The choice between beam search and sampling depends on whether your task prioritizes optimal output quality (beam search) or creative diversity (sampling).
keyPoints: Explores multiple candidate sequences simultaneously at each step
Beam width B controls the exploration vs computation trade-off
B=1 is equivalent to greedy decoding
Best for translation and summarisation; sampling is preferred for creative tasks

terms.name: Greedy Decoding
terms.tagline: The simplest decoding strategy - at each step, always choose the token with the highest probability
terms.plainEnglish:
Greedy decoding is the most straightforward way to generate text from a language model. At each step, it picks the single token with the highest probability - the model's "best guess." No randomness, no exploration, no second-guessing. It's equivalent to setting temperature to 0 or top-K to 1. The same prompt always produces the same output, which makes greedy decoding fully deterministic.

Greedy decoding is fast and simple, but it has significant drawbacks. First, it can get stuck in repetitive loops - once the model assigns high probability to a repeating pattern, greedy decoding will stay in that rut. Second, it often misses better overall sequences because a locally suboptimal choice might lead to a much better continuation. Third, it produces bland text compared to sampling methods because it always picks the most obvious token.

Despite these flaws, greedy decoding is useful for evaluation (to ensure reproducibility), for tasks requiring maximum predictability (like extracting structured data), and as a baseline to compare against more sophisticated decoding strategies. Most production applications, however, use sampling-based methods for higher-quality output.
terms.analogy: Greedy decoding is like hiking a mountain by always taking the single steepest step upward from where you stand. Each individual step gets you closer to higher ground. But this strategy might lead you up a cliff face when a slightly gentler first step would have led to an easy path around the cliff. You never looked ahead - you just took the best immediate step. In hiking (and in text generation), the locally optimal choice isn't always the globally optimal one.
terms.howItWorks:
At each generation step t, the model outputs a probability distribution P(token | tokens[0..t-1]) over all tokens in the vocabulary. Greedy decoding selects argmax(P) - the token with the highest probability. This token is appended to the sequence, and the process repeats. The complexity per step is O(V) - just finding the maximum value in the output vector.

Because greedy decoding always takes the highest-probability path, it's equivalent to beam search with beam width = 1. It's also equivalent to setting temperature to 0 in the sampling framework (though some implementations use a special case rather than actual division by zero).

The mathematical weakness of greedy decoding is that it optimizes for the best token at each individual step rather than the best overall sequence. This is a classic greedy algorithm limitation - it makes locally optimal choices that don't guarantee a globally optimal result. In text generation, this means greedy decoding tends to produce shorter, more generic, and sometimes repetitive output.
terms.inPractice:
Greedy decoding is commonly used in evaluation benchmarks where reproducibility is essential. When comparing model performance, you want to eliminate the randomness of sampling to get clean metrics. It's also used in production for tasks where you need deterministic output - extracting structured data (JSON, entities), controlling costs with short responses, or generating predictable system messages.

For most creative and conversational applications, however, greedy decoding produces noticeably worse output than sampling methods with appropriate temperature. The difference is especially pronounced in open-ended generation: greedy decoding tends to produce flat, boring text, while sampling produces more natural, interesting, and diverse language. The recommendation for most applications is to use temperature 0.7-0.9 with top-P 0.9-0.95 rather than greedy decoding.
keyPoints: Always picks the single most likely token at each step -- deterministic
Fast but can miss better global sequences
Equivalent to temperature 0 or beam search with beam width 1
Good for evaluation and structured output; poor for creative writing
